title: Born-Qualified: An Autonomous Framework for Deploying Advanced Energy and Electronic Materials
authors: Steven R. Spurgeon, Milad Abolhasani, Frederick Baddour, Ryan B. Comes, Vinayak P. Dravid, Hilary Egan, Patrick Emami, Robert W. Epps, Davi M. Fébba, Renae Gannon, E. Ashley Gaulding, Ayana Ghosh, Kenny Gruchalla, Grace Guinan, Taro Hitosugi, Michael Holden, Sergei V. Kalinin, Yangang Liang, John S. Mangum, Matthew J. Olszta, Nathaniel H. Park, Axel Palmstrom, Michelle A. Smeaton, Brooks Tellekamp, Nicholas E. Thornburg, Raymond R. Unocic, Daniela Ushizima, Rama K. Vasudevan, Robert White, Andrew Young, Andriy Zakutayev
abstract: Autonomous science is transforming how we discover materials and chemical systems for advanced energy technologies. However, many initially promising systems never reach deployment. This "valley of death" stems from optimization that prioritizes laboratory metrics over industrial viability. We propose a new strategy: "born-qualified" autonomous development, which embeds manufacturability, cost, and durability constraints from the outset. This approach is enabled by four pillars, including the development of multi-objective metrics, causal models, a modular infrastructure, and embedding manufacturing in the discovery loop. Realizing this vision will require sustained, community-wide commitment, but the potential return on that investment is commensurate with the scale of the challenge.
summary: This paper proposes a new strategy for autonomous materials discovery called 'Born-Qualified,' which aims to bridge the gap between lab breakthroughs and industrial use. By embedding manufacturing, cost, and durability constraints into the AI-driven discovery loop from the start, the framework seeks to prevent the development of high-performance materials that are impossible to scale. This approach could significantly accelerate the deployment of next-generation energy and electronic technologies.
main_problem: A significant 'valley of death' exists between the laboratory discovery of advanced materials and their industrial deployment due to a mismatch where optimization prioritizes lab-scale performance over manufacturability, cost, and durability.
main_result: The authors propose the 'Born-Qualified' framework, a new paradigm for autonomous science that integrates manufacturing, cost, and stability constraints directly into the initial discovery loop.
method: The framework utilizes four pillars: multi-objective metrics, causal models, modular infrastructure, and the integration of manufacturing into the discovery loop, employing AI/ML, LLMs, and closed-loop automation.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to various energy and electronic material systems, including lithium-sulfur and lithium-ion batteries, photovoltaics (GaAs, Perovskite, CdTe), and power electronics (Si, SiC, GaN, Diamond).
key_observables: Laboratory metrics (catalytic activity, ionic conductivity, band gap, specific capacity, ZT) and industrial metrics (scalability, cost-effectiveness, durability, and process compatibility).
important_parameters: Performance vs. deployability trade-offs, scale-up from milligrams to industrial quantities, and the transition from laboratory-scale to pilot-scale manufacturing.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes that integrating manufacturing constraints early can overcome the limitations of current sequential discovery models and that AI-driven automation can handle multi-objective optimization.
figures_summary: Figure 1 correlates the performance gap between lab records and commercial products with market penetration; Figure 2 contrasts the traditional sequential discovery approach with the proposed continuous 'Born-Qualified' approach.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the discovery-deployment gap, proposes the 'Born-Qualified' strategy via four enabling pillars, details the necessary experimental and computational infrastructure, and provides case studies in power electronics and energy storage.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The Discovery–Deployment Gap in Energy and Electronic Technologies. (a–c) Bars compare commercial product performance, laboratory system and component records, and theoretical limits for key photovoltaic, battery, and power electronic technologies. Percentages indicate estimated market share. X/SSB/Y denotes solid-state battery; electrode chemistry varies by formulation and is detailed in the accompanying data file. NiMH = nickel-metal hydride.

Figure 2: Traditional Vs. Born-Qualified Discovery. Comparison of sequential and born-qualified approaches, showing how a linear decision-making process fails to anticipate challenges in scale-up, while a multi-objective approach can more effectively produce feasible designs.
